items.front: 5-1-1: Does a successful commissioning flight check relieve the controller of the personal satisfaction requirement?
items.back: No. The controller must still be personally satisfied that the radar presentation and equipment performance are adequate for the service being provided.

items.front: 5-1-1: The note says radar services are not limited to commissioning flight check parameters. What does this mean in practice?
items.back: Radar services may be provided beyond the distance and altitude parameters obtained during the commissioning check, as long as the controller remains personally satisfied with adequacy.

items.front: 5-1-2: Three conditions for secondary radar as sole display source outside Class A airspace
items.back: 1. Additional coverage beyond primary radar. 2. Primary radar temporarily unusable/out of service AND pilots advised. 3. Secondary radar is the only source of radar data for the area AND terminal pilots advised.

items.front: 5-1-2: TERMINAL — when does the standard source-use restriction still apply in FUSION mode?
items.back: Only when required by facility directive.

items.front: 5-1-2: TERMINAL — secondary-radar-only ASR final approaches
items.back: Prohibited unless an emergency EXISTS AND the pilot CONCURS.

items.front: 5-1-2: STARS modes that permit ADS-B and WAM targets for all terminal services
items.back: STARS Fusion, STARS FMA, and STARS Multi-Sensor Mode. Includes services for procedures annotated 'radar required.'

items.front: 5-1-2: WAM limitation in the EAS
items.back: WAM-derived targets CANNOT be used to provide 3 NM separation in the EAS. 3 NM targets are not derived from WAM within the EAS.

items.front: 5-1-3: EA activity types recognized in stop/resume requests
items.back: Chaff dispensing: 'stream' or 'burst.' Electronic jamming: 'buzzer.'

items.front: 5-1-3: EN ROUTE vs TERMINAL — resuming suspended EA activity
items.back: EN ROUTE: inform NORAD unit or aircraft. TERMINAL: inform ARTCC or aircraft; MUST obtain ARTCC approval before broadcasting resume directly to aircraft.

items.front: 5-1-3: Four elements in every EA stop request
items.back: 1. Facility name. 2. Type of EA activity (stream/burst or buzzer). 3. Radar band affected. 4. When feasible, expected duration of suspension.

items.front: 5-1-3: Who should be consulted before approving/disapproving EA activity that could affect narrowband radar?
items.back: The Systems Engineer, concerning the effect of EA on the operational use of the narrowband radar.

items.front: 5-1-4: Three aircraft categories receiving merging target procedures (except while in holding)
items.back: 1. Radar-identified aircraft at 10,000 feet and above. 2. All turbojet aircraft regardless of altitude. 3. Presidential aircraft regardless of altitude.

items.front: 5-1-4: RVSM merging target trigger — two aircraft, 1,000 ft vertical separation
items.back: Use vectors to prevent merging when EITHER pilot reports unable RVSM due to turbulence or mountain wave. ('Unable RVSM' phrase intended only for severe turbulence with altitude deviations of approximately 200 ft or more.)

items.front: 5-1-4: Pilot requests vector to avoid merging — controller unable to provide
items.back: Must inform the pilot that vector service cannot be provided. The obligation to inform is unconditional.

items.front: 5-1-4: Why issue traffic information early in merging target situations?
items.back: Closure rates can be rapid; the pilot needs enough time to decide whether a vector is necessary.

items.front: 5-1-5: Scope of holding pattern surveillance — which holding areas?
items.back: Outer-fix holding pattern airspace areas, or portions thereof, shown on the radar scope (displayed on video map or scribed on map overlay). Not all holding areas — only depicted outer-fix ones.

items.front: 5-1-5: Three-part holding surveillance obligation
items.back: 1. Provide surveillance when aircraft are holding. 2. Attempt to detect aircraft straying outside the area. 3. If stray detected, assist return to assigned airspace.

items.front: 5-1-6: RNAV ATS route dimensions
items.back: Total width: 8 NM. Laterally protected airspace: 4 NM each side of centerline. Aircraft navigation performance requirement: capable of remaining within 2 NM of centerline.

items.front: 5-1-6: What does an aircraft approaching the 2 NM centerline limit on an RNAV route indicate?
items.back: May be experiencing a navigation system error or failure.

items.front: 5-1-6: Deviation advisory threshold
items.back: When the aircraft is observed in a position and on a track that will OBVIOUSLY cause deviation from protected airspace. Not speculative — the deviation must be obvious.

items.front: 5-1-7: EN ROUTE — trigger for manual fix posting
items.back: EAS FDP flight progress recording components are NOT operational. If they are operational, the requirement does NOT apply.

items.front: 5-1-7: Scope and minimum frequency of manual fix posting
items.back: EN ROUTE only. At least ONCE for EACH controlled aircraft in the sector of responsibility.

items.front: 5-1-8: Effect of 'radar contact' on position reporting
items.back: Aircraft DISCONTINUES reporting over compulsory reporting points. Resumes normal position reporting ONLY when ATC states 'radar contact lost' or 'radar service terminated.'

items.front: 5-1-8: Can a controller still request a position report after 'radar contact' is established?
items.back: Yes. If necessary, the controller may request an estimate or report over a specific fix at any time.

items.front: 5-1-9: General rule for radar service termination
items.back: Inform aircraft when radar service is terminated. Phraseology: 'RADAR SERVICE TERMINATED' (nonradar routing if required).

items.front: 5-1-9: Five situations where radar service is AUTOMATICALLY terminated (no advisory needed)
items.back: 1. IFR cancellation outside Class B/C/TRSA/basic-radar areas. 2. Aircraft on instrument/visual/contact approach has landed or changed to advisory frequency. 3. (NOT automatic — see exception at airport with < 1/2 mile radar coverage.) 4. TERMINAL VFR to Class B/C/TRSA/basic-radar airport has landed, or to other airports changed to tower/advisory. 5. TERMINAL aircraft completes radar approach.

items.front: 5-1-9: IFR cancellation — when is a separate termination advisory STILL required?
items.back: When the cancellation occurs WITHIN Class B, Class C, TRSA, or where basic radar service is provided. These areas are EXCEPTED from the automatic termination rule.

items.front: 5-1-9: Tower-controlled airport radar coverage exception for termination advisory
items.back: At airports where radar coverage does NOT exist to within 1/2 mile of the runway end, arriving aircraft MUST be informed when radar service is terminated — the automatic termination rules do not excuse the advisory.
